summary.difficultyAssessment: D1:D2:D3 distribution is 60:97:108 which is 23%:37%:41%. D3 is significantly over-represented (41% vs 30% target) while D1 is under-represented (23% vs 30% target). The D2 bucket is slightly below target (37% vs 40%). The heavy D3 skew is driven by Q46-Q130 — a block of 80+ D3 questions that are structurally identical (large dividend / 2-digit divisor, exact division, tightly clustered consecutive options). Many of these D3 questions are single-step exact divisions that could be D2 with a different divisor range. D1 questions are well-calibrated — single-digit or friendly divisors, 2-3 digit dividends, times-table level.
summary.coverageAssessment: Strong coverage of GL question types 1 (equal sharing/grouping), 2 (remainder interpretation), 5 (estimation/checking), and 7 (multi-step). However, Type 3 (inverse/missing number — 'what number multiplied by X gives Y?') is completely absent. Type 5 (division with decimals/money giving decimal answers) is also absent — all money questions divide exactly. Type 6 (working backwards — 'a number divided by X gives Y remainder Z, what is the number?') is completely absent. These three missing types are all GL-tested skills flagged in the research. The 'zero in the quotient' trap (e.g. 2016/8 where you must write 0) is not explicitly tested. The question bank is heavily dominated by single-step exact divisions.
summary.teachingEfficacyAssessment: 6 sub-concepts with 12 lessons provide adequate coverage of core division skills. The sharing-equally, short-division-remainders, long-division-method, and interpreting-remainders sub-concepts are well-taught with BusStopDiagram and WorkedExample visuals. However, the 'master-bus-stop' sub-concept is mapped to 20 D1 questions but has NO lesson content ([No lesson found]). The estimation-checking lessons are good but only mapped to 22 questions. The dividing-word-problems lesson is solid but carries a heavy load (mapped to many questions). Overall, lessons are warm, age-appropriate, and use good pedagogical patterns (curiosity hooks, spot-the-mistake, step-by-step). The main gap is the missing lesson for master-bus-stop.
topPriorities: Fix Q172 wrong answer (critical: answer is 20 not 21 — explanation contradicts itself)
Add inverse/missing-number questions and working-backwards questions — two GL-tested types completely absent
Fix D1:D2:D3 distribution — need ~20 more D1 questions and ~30 fewer D3, or re-classify some D3 single-step exact divisions as D2

issues.title: Wrong answer: 250 / 12 = 20 remainder 10, but answer marked as 21
issues.description: Q172 asks 'Chairs are arranged in rows of 12 for a school assembly. There are 250 children. How many full rows are there?' The correct answer is 20 full rows (12 x 20 = 240, remainder 10). The marked answer is 21 (option C, index 2). The explanation even states 'remainder 10' and then contradictorily says '20 full rows' but also claims the remaining '10 children sit in an incomplete 21st row, but only 20 rows are full'. Despite the explanation correctly stating 20, the correctAnswer field says '21' and correct index is 2 (pointing to 21). The correct answer should be 20 (index 1).
issues.suggestedFix: Change correct from 2 to 1, and correctAnswer from '21' to '20'. The explanation is actually correct — just the answer index is wrong.

issues.title: D3 over-represented at 41% (target 30%), D1 under-represented at 23% (target 30%)
issues.description: Current distribution is D1:60(23%), D2:97(37%), D3:108(41%). GL target is approximately 30:40:30. The D3 block (Q46-Q130) contains 80+ questions that follow an identical pattern: large 4-5 digit dividend divided by a 2-digit divisor (80-99 range) giving an exact quotient. While the divisors are large, these are all single-step exact divisions — the only thing making them D3 is the size of the numbers. Per the GL research, D3 should involve multi-step problems, disguised division, remainder interpretation in non-obvious ways, working backwards, or decimal division — not just bigger numbers.
issues.suggestedFix: Reclassify approximately 20-30 of the single-step exact-division D3 questions (those with divisors 60-80 that divide exactly) as D2. Add 15-20 genuinely D1 questions using 1-digit divisors with smaller dividends. Alternatively, replace some of the repetitive D3 block with genuinely harder question types (multi-step, working backwards, decimal).

issues.title: No inverse/missing-number questions (GL Type 3)
issues.description: The GL research identifies 'inverse / missing number' as a common division question type: 'What number multiplied by 23 gives 621?', '_ x 15 = 450', 'I think of a number, multiply by 8, and get 296. What was my number?' None of the 265 questions test this skill. This is a significant gap because it tests conceptual understanding of division as inverse of multiplication, which GL explicitly assesses.
issues.suggestedFix: Add 8-10 inverse/missing-number questions across D1-D3. D1: '_ x 7 = 63', D2: 'What number times 23 gives 621?', D3: 'I think of a number, multiply by 17, then add 45. My answer is 249. What was my number?'

issues.title: No working-backwards questions (GL Type 6)
issues.description: The GL research identifies 'working backwards' as a key D3 question type: 'A number divided by 14 gives 23 remainder 8. What is the number?' (answer: 14 x 23 + 8 = 330). This requires understanding the division algorithm (dividend = divisor x quotient + remainder). None of the 265 questions test this. It is specifically listed as a GL trap (Trap 7) where children forget to add the remainder.
issues.suggestedFix: Add 5-8 working-backwards questions at D2-D3 difficulty. E.g. 'When I divide my number by 25, I get exactly 36. What is my number?', 'A number divided by 14 gives 23 remainder 8. What is the number?'

issues.title: No decimal-answer division questions (GL Type 5)
issues.description: Every money question in the bank divides exactly to a whole-pound answer. GL tests questions like 'Four friends share a restaurant bill of £97.60 equally. How much does each pay?' (£24.40) or 'A piece of wood is 3.78m long. It is cut into 6 equal pieces. How long is each piece?' (0.63m). The absence of decimal-answer questions means children never practise this GL-tested skill.
issues.suggestedFix: Add 5-8 questions at D2-D3 where the division produces a decimal answer, particularly in money and measurement contexts. E.g. '£45.60 shared among 8 children = £5.70', 'A 3.78m plank cut into 6 equal pieces = 0.63m'.

issues.title: 85 D3 questions follow identical pattern with consecutive-integer options
issues.description: Questions 46-130 overwhelmingly follow the same template: '[Entity] has [large number] [items] to [share/pack/divide] into [groups of large 2-digit number]. How many [groups]?' Every single one has 5 consecutive integers as options (e.g. 40,41,42,43,44 or 55,56,57,58,59). While consecutive-integer distractors are a valid GL pattern (Pattern 6 in the research), using ONLY this pattern for 85 straight questions eliminates variety. GL also uses off-by-one errors, remainder mishandling, place-value errors, and wrong-operation distractors. The correct answer is almost always at index 2 (the middle option) — see answer position issue below.
issues.suggestedFix: Vary distractor patterns: include some options with wider spacing (e.g. 20, 25, 30, 35, 40), some with a remainder-mishandling trap, and some where the correct answer is NOT the middle option. Also vary the question structure — not all should be single-step exact divisions.

issues.title: Extreme answer position bias — correct answer is option C (index 2) in ~80% of questions
issues.description: Of the 265 questions, approximately 210+ have the correct answer at index 2 (option C, the middle of 5 options). This is a severe position bias. GL Assessment distributes answers roughly evenly across A-E. A child who simply always picks C would score anomalously high on this topic. In the original Q1-Q130 block, virtually every answer is at index 2. The v2 questions (Q131+) introduce slightly more variety but still heavily favour index 1 and 2.
issues.suggestedFix: Redistribute correct answer positions to be roughly 20% per position (A through E). This requires adjusting the option sets so the correct answer appears at different positions while maintaining plausible distractors.

issues.title: No questions testing 'zero in the quotient' — a key GL trap
issues.description: The GL research identifies 'zero in the quotient' as Trap 2 and the single most common procedural error in long division. Example: 3024 / 14 = 216, or 2040 / 12 = 170 where the zero IS part of the answer. None of the 265 questions have a quotient containing a zero (like 102, 204, 305, etc. where the zero must be written). Questions Q110 and Q113 have answers of 101, but these involve 4-digit dividends divided by 92/96 — they don't specifically test the zero-in-quotient trap where children might skip the zero.
issues.suggestedFix: Add 4-6 questions where the quotient contains a zero, specifically designed to expose this error. E.g. '3024 / 14 = ?' with distractors including 216, 26, 2016, 2160 (common zero-error answers). Include both D2 and D3 versions.

issues.title: master-bus-stop sub-concept has no lesson content — 20 questions orphaned
issues.description: The mapping file shows 20 D1 questions mapped to 'master-bus-stop' where lessonTeachSummary is '[No lesson found]' and learningGoal is empty. These include Q1, Q6, Q10, and many v2 D1 questions (Q131-Q154). Children answering these questions have no micro-lesson to prepare them. The 'sharing-equally' sub-concept has lessons but is only mapped to basic sharing questions, not to bus-stop-method D1 questions like '156 / 12 = 13'.
issues.suggestedFix: Either create a lesson for the master-bus-stop sub-concept covering the bus stop (short division) method for 1-digit and friendly 2-digit divisors, OR remap these questions to the existing sharing-equally or short-division-remainders sub-concepts as appropriate.

issues.title: Long division lesson teach summary missing from mapping — shows '[No lesson found]'
issues.description: The long-division-method sub-concept has 2 lessons but the mapping shows lessonTeachSummary as '[No lesson found]' for all questions mapped to it (Q2, Q5, Q7, Q8, Q11, Q12, Q15-Q19, etc.). The lesson exists in the lesson data but the mapping isn't picking up the teach summary. This suggests a mapping extraction issue rather than a content issue. The lesson itself is good — clear step-by-step BusStopDiagram walkthrough of 432 / 18 with EMSB (Estimate, Multiply, Subtract, Bring down) cycle.
issues.suggestedFix: Fix the mapping extraction so lessonTeachSummary populates for long-division-method. The lesson content itself is fine.

issues.title: Multiple consolidate screens have no body content
issues.description: Several consolidate screens across the lessons have null body and no bodyParts, visual, or interaction — they would render as blank screens. Affected lessons: short-division-remainders-steps (line 488), short-division-remainders-mistake (line 620), long-division-method-steps (line 883), long-division-method-mistake (line 1047), interpreting-remainders-mistake (line 1345). The consolidate screen is meant to reinforce the key takeaway — a blank screen wastes this opportunity.
issues.suggestedFix: Add a brief summary body to each empty consolidate screen. E.g. for short-division-remainders: 'When the number doesn't divide exactly, the bit left over is the **remainder**. Always check: the remainder must be **smaller** than the number you're dividing by!'

issues.title: Q155 rated D2 but uses single-digit divisor (7) — should be D1
issues.description: Q155: 'A charity has 168 selection boxes to share equally among 7 children's homes. How many boxes does each home receive?' 168 / 7 = 24. Dividing by 7 with a 3-digit dividend is solidly within times-table recall range (7 x 24 = 168). Per the GL research, D1 includes division by single-digit numbers up to 3-digit dividends with no remainder. This is D1.
issues.suggestedFix: Reclassify Q155 from D2 to D1.

issues.title: Q156 rated D2 but uses single-digit divisor (9) — should be D1
issues.description: Q156: 'A scout leader shares 252 badges equally among 9 scout groups.' 252 / 9 = 28. Dividing by 9 with a 3-digit dividend and no remainder is D1 per the GL criteria (single-digit divisor, straightforward sharing).
issues.suggestedFix: Reclassify Q156 from D2 to D1.

issues.title: Q157 rated D2 but uses single-digit divisor (8) — should be D1
issues.description: Q157: 'A farmer shares 336 eggs equally among 8 shops.' 336 / 8 = 42. Same pattern — single-digit divisor, 3-digit dividend, exact division. This is D1.
issues.suggestedFix: Reclassify Q157 from D2 to D1.

issues.title: Q46 rated D3 but is a straightforward single-step division — should be D2
issues.description: Q46: 'A school raises £1,927 at a fair and shares it equally among 47 charities. How many pounds does each charity receive?' 1927 / 47 = 41. While 47 is a non-friendly 2-digit divisor and the dividend is 4-digits, this is a single-step exact division with no remainder interpretation, no multi-step reasoning, no disguised operation. Per the GL research, D3 requires multi-step, non-obvious remainder interpretation, decimal answers, working backwards, or disguised division. This fits D2 criteria: 2-digit divisor, 4-digit dividend, requires formal long division method.
issues.suggestedFix: Reclassify Q46 from D3 to D2. This applies to many questions in the Q46-Q70 range that share the same single-step pattern with divisors in the 40-70 range. A systematic reclassification pass would move about 20-25 of these to D2.

issues.title: Checking/estimation questions have explanations that give away more than needed
issues.description: Several estimation and checking questions (Q180, Q182, Q185, Q187, etc.) include the full correct calculation in the explanation, which is fine for review. However, some explanations reveal the correct answer to a DIFFERENT calculation than what was asked. For example, Q185 asks 'explain why 891/9 = 89 must be wrong' — the explanation says 'the correct answer is 99' which goes beyond the question. Q182 asks 'Is 576/24 = 26 correct?' and the explanation gives the full correct answer of 24. These explanations are correct and educational, but could be more focused on the checking METHOD rather than giving the full answer.
issues.suggestedFix: Minor tone refinement: lead with the checking method ('89 x 9 = 801, not 891 — so the answer is wrong') before stating the correct answer. This reinforces the checking skill rather than just giving the answer.
